queries.query: how should database schema migrations be rolled back safely in production

memories.content: Every schema migration must ship with a tested down-migration; the March prod incident was an irreversible ALTER with no recovery path that forced a 40-minute outage.
Migrations run against a staging clone of prod data with a rehearsed rollback before promotion.
Down-migrations are optional; if a change breaks we just roll forward with a hotfix and never revert schema.
A migration that can't be reversed cleanly is gated behind a feature flag so reads can switch back instantly.
We snapshot the affected tables immediately before a high-risk migration as a last-resort recovery point.
Long backfills are chunked and run out-of-band so the migration transaction stays short.
Expand-and-contract is mandatory for column changes: add the new column, dual-write, backfill, switch reads, then drop the old column in a later release.
A separate change to rollback touched the same area but addressed downtime rather than the question of how to how should database schema migrations be rolled .

Our ORM auto-generates migration files, but the generated names are hard to read in review.

queries.query: how do we decide which database indexes to add for a slow query

memories.content: Add indexes from the query plan, not by guessing: run EXPLAIN ANALYZE, index the columns in the WHERE/JOIN that show sequential scans, and order composite indexes by selectivity.
Indexes never hurt; add one on every column that appears in any query to be safe.
Unused indexes are dropped quarterly based on pg_stat_user_indexes scan counts.
Every new index is justified by a measured plan improvement and weighed against write amplification, since each index slows inserts and updates.
Someone mentioned a partial index might help the dashboard query, but it was never benchmarked.

Consumers of a shared table are tracked in the service registry so a deprecation can be announced to every owning team before a breaking change.
Each shared table has a documented owner who reviews proposed schema changes.
Just rename the column and fix the consumers in the same PR; coordinating deprecations is overkill.

queries.query: when should we partition or shard a large and growing table

memories.content: Partition by time-range once a table passes ~100M rows and queries are time-bounded; reach for sharding only when a single node can't hold the working set, and pick a shard key that spreads load to avoid hot partitions.
Before sharding we exhaust vertical scaling and read replicas, since sharding is hard to reverse.
The shard key must match the dominant access pattern or every query becomes a scatter-gather.
Range partitions are created ahead of time by a scheduled job so inserts never hit a missing partition.

Always shard from day one; designing for a single database is a rookie mistake regardless of size.

queries.query: how should we size database connection pools and timeouts under load

memories.content: Pool size should be bounded well below the database's max_connections and sized to cores, not request concurrency; the last brownout came from each pod opening 50 connections and saturating Postgres, fixed by a small pool plus a short acquire timeout and backpressure.
Total connections = pods × pool size must stay under the database ceiling with headroom for migrations.
A short connection-acquire timeout sheds load fast instead of letting requests queue forever.

queries.query: what caused the authentication outage and how was it remediated

memories.content: The auth outage was a token clock-skew bug: nodes drifted, so freshly issued JWTs failed not-before validation; the fix pins NTP and widens the nbf/exp tolerance to 60 seconds.
The outage was a database failover, not clock skew; tokens were never involved.
Auth nodes now sync time from a hardened NTP source with drift monitoring on each host.
A cold auth cache now falls back to short-lived refresh tokens instead of rejecting the request.
The postmortem action items track widening tolerance, NTP pinning, and a skew dashboard.
Token validation clock tolerance is a tunable config so we can widen it without a redeploy.

queries.query: how do we prevent retry storms and cascading failures between services

memories.content: Retries must use exponential backoff with jitter and a bounded budget, fronted by a circuit breaker; the checkout cascade happened because synchronous retries with no jitter amplified a downstream blip into a thundering herd.
Circuit breakers open on sustained error rates and shed load instead of retrying into a dead dependency.
Retries are only allowed on idempotent operations to avoid duplicate side effects.
A separate change to retry touched the same area but addressed exponential backoff rather than the question of how to how do we prevent retry storms and cascading fai.
Every cross-service call has a timeout shorter than its caller's so timeout budgets nest correctly.

Disable circuit breakers; they cause more outages than they prevent by failing requests early.

queries.query: how should application secrets and credentials be stored and rotated

memories.content: Secrets live in the managed secret manager, injected at runtime and never committed; they are rotated on a schedule and immediately on suspected exposure, after a key was once leaked in a committed .env file.
On a suspected leak, the runbook is to rotate the credential first and investigate second.
Production secrets are never readable by developers directly; access goes through the secret manager.
Secret access is scoped per service and audit-logged so a leak's blast radius is bounded.
Short-lived dynamically issued credentials are preferred over long-lived static keys.
Pre-commit and CI secret scanning block credentials from entering the repo.

queries.query: what are the constraints for issuing and validating JWT access tokens securely

memories.content: JWTs must be signed with an asymmetric algorithm (never 'none' or a shared HMAC secret), carry short expiries plus audience and issuer claims, and pin the expected alg; we had a near-miss where alg:none would have been accepted.
Long-lived non-expiring JWTs are fine as long as they're signed; revocation isn't needed.
Access tokens are short-lived and paired with revocable refresh tokens for logout and compromise.
Signing keys are rotated via a JWKS endpoint with overlapping key validity.

queries.query: how should we validate and sanitize untrusted input to prevent injection

memories.content: Treat all external input as untrusted: use parameterized queries for SQL, context-aware output encoding for HTML, and allowlist validation at the boundary; the reported bug was string-built SQL that allowed injection.
A web application firewall handles injection, so input validation in code is redundant.
Output encoding is chosen by the sink — HTML, attribute, URL, or JS context — to stop XSS.
Validation is allowlist-based (what is permitted) rather than blocklist-based (what is forbidden).

queries.query: what retrieval strategy best supports long-horizon agent memory

memories.content: Long-horizon recall should weight importance and recency alongside semantic similarity so critical-but-distant memories surface; similarity-only retrieval buries an important decision under many superficially similar but low-value notes.

Importance is a stored signal on each memory so the ranker doesn't have to infer it at query time.
Retrieval is evaluated on recall and ranking, not just top-1 similarity.

queries.query: how do we make agent evaluation runs reproducible and replayable

memories.content: Every run must persist enough metadata — model, prompt, context package, and seed — to replay it later against new strategies; reproducibility is a first-class requirement, so the luck roll is seeded by (agent, case, trial) and never by the strategy under test.
A separate change to reproducible touched the same area but addressed replay rather than the question of how to how do we make agent evaluation runs reproducibl.
Stored run records link the case, context version, and agent run for deterministic replay.
A separate change to replay touched the same area but addressed seed rather than the question of how to how do we make agent evaluation runs reproducibl.
Synthetic results are flagged as non-production so they aren't mistaken for real evidence.
[2024] Just rerun the agent; if results differ, average a few runs and move on.

queries.query: how should the eval platform stay decoupled from execution and retrieval providers

memories.content: Execution and retrieval must sit behind provider interfaces with mock and real MCP implementations that are drop-in equivalents; the eval platform measures outcomes and must not absorb workspace management or context generation that belong to other systems.
Retrieval/memory logic stays behind the Cortex provider, not inside the eval engine.
The eval platform should run the agent and generate context itself to avoid coordinating with other teams.

Mock providers let the full pipeline run end-to-end without a live backend.
Real MCP providers are selected from the environment and fall back to mocks independently.

queries.query: what guidelines keep pull request diffs small and focused

memories.content: A PR must touch only files required by its task; unrelated edits, drive-by refactors, and whitespace churn are rejected in review because they hide the real change and bloat the diff.
Large changes are split into a stack of small, independently reviewable PRs.
Reviewers approve faster when the diff is small, so focus is also a velocity win.
Mechanical reformatting goes in its own commit, separate from behavior changes.
Reviewers flag trailing-newline and reformat-only changes as noise that obscures intent.
A separate change to focused touched the same area but addressed scope creep rather than the question of how to what guidelines keep pull request diffs small an.
A separate change to diff touched the same area but addressed focused rather than the question of how to what guidelines keep pull request diffs small an.
A PR that touches files outside the task's scope is sent back to trim the unrelated edits.
Maybe our guideline caps PRs at 400 lines, but the number might have changed.
[2021] Big PRs are fine if they're well described; reviewers should just read more carefully.
A separate change to scope creep touched the same area but addressed whitespace rather than the question of how to what guidelines keep pull request diffs small an.
Bundle unrelated cleanups into the same PR; it saves opening multiple reviews.
We squash-merge so the main branch has one commit per PR.
The PR template lists a checklist of sections to fill in before requesting review.

queries.query: what testing is required before a change can be merged

memories.content: A change merges only with tests covering the new behavior and a regression test for any bug it fixes; CI must be green, and a bug fix without a failing-then-passing test is sent back.
CI runs the full suite as a required merge gate, not an optional check.
Coverage percentage is all that matters; a high number means the change is safe to merge untested.
Bug fixes add a test that fails before the fix and passes after, proving the fix works.

Review feedback references the task's acceptance criteria, not personal preference.

memories.content: Names follow the surrounding file's conventions rather than a contributor's personal habit.

New code should read like the code around it: match the existing module's naming, idioms, and structure, with automated formatting and linting enforcing the mechanical rules so reviews focus on substance.
Public APIs follow documented naming conventions for discoverability.

queries.query: what should a pull request description contain to be reviewable

memories.content: A PR description states what changed and why, links the task and its acceptance criteria, and notes how it was tested; reviewers can't evaluate intent without the why, so a description that only restates the diff is sent back.
Linking the originating issue lets the reviewer check the change against its acceptance criteria.
Out-of-scope follow-ups are called out so they aren't expected in this PR.
